If during the application and/or selection process you require accommodation due to a disability, please contact Diane Giang at director.engsci@utoronto.ca.
duties:
The Division of Engineering Science is looking for an instructor to teach ESC180 H1F.  The instructors will be responsible for:

Preparing and delivering lecture content, ideally incorporating active learning pedagogy; preparing in-laboratory activities to help students to solidify their understanding of course materials; preparing and developing assessment tools for independent or group assignments that integrate across course materials;  preparing and developing assessment tools for closely supervised tests (preparation of marking rubrics, term test/s, final exam); assessing/grading student work.  Hiring and managing Teaching Assistants for the course including mentoring, and providing necessary training as per the CUPE 3902 Unit 1 Memorandum of Agreement. Hold weekly office hour, and attend practical sessions as needed.
experience: The need to acquire experience is the more relevant criterion than past teaching experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum: Open to graduate students who have an APSC B.A.Sc. or equivalent. Excellent programming skills in Python and C (with an emphasis on bit manipulation, pointers, and memory management). Thorough and fluent understanding of the Python and C memory models and complexity analysis. Excellent skills in designing recursive functions. Excellent communication skills when talking about programming and algorithms.  Must have served as a Teaching Assistant (or equivalent) or Course Instructor for at least one introductory programming course, ESC180H1 and/or ESC190H1; must be familiar with implementing data structures including linked lists, hash tables, queues, stacks, trees, graphs, heaps, etc., and their analysis; must be familiar with algorithms e.g. sorting, searching, graph traversal, etc., and their analysis; should be familiar with contemporary introductory programming pedagogy.
qualifications_preferred: Engineering Science graduates preferred. Enthusiasm for teaching beginners.

department: Undergraduate Medical Education (Temerty Faculty of Medicine)
duties:
Responsibilities include, but are not limited to, the following:  

Supervising in-person assessments (i.e. Mastery Exercises and Progress Tests) across the MD Program; some remote shifts may be required.

Through active invigilation, maintain the integrity of assessments by adhering to faculty and university policies and procedures. 

Maintaining confidentiality and security of student and assessment information.

Monitoring test/exam time allotments; Handling student concerns/problems and referring to MD Program staff where appropriate.

Reporting anomalous situations

Verifying student identification.

Making assessment related announcements to students.

In certain cases, invigilators may assist in transporting backup laptops between the Medical Sciences Building and the Exam Center at 255 McCaul Street or another exam site on the St. George campus.

Invigilators are required to attend an in-person orientation session for a mock Mastery Exercise August 24, 2026.
qualifications: Applicants must be currently registered as students pursuing a graduate degree with the School of Graduate Studies at the University of Toronto; Applicants must be available for in-person invigilation shifts between August 24, 2026 and June 30, 2027; Applicants must be available for virtual orientation week of August 17, 2026; Experience invigilating tests/exams is an asset; Understanding and ability to apply relevant University and government policies, procedures and legislation.

job_title: Family Practice Across the Life Cycle: Caregiving and Older Adults
application_procedure: Please apply by email, with a cover letter and curriculum vitae to angela.umbrello@utoronto.ca
duties: Teaching a course titled “Family Practice Across the Life Cycle: Caregiving and Older Adults” based on the approved course outline.  The instructor must adhere to course policies on teaching and holding the course at the scheduled time.  The course content, notes, readings, and assignments are uniform across all sections.  The teaching team meets regularly to discuss teaching and learning activities and instructors are expected to attend these meetings. For all assignments and final grades, instructors are expected to meet established deadlines. Quercus management: including learning and utilizing Quercus; the marking and grading of student work and the submission of grades to the course coordinator by the required date; adhering to the policies on teaching and holding classes when scheduled; the maintenance of reasonable hours of student contact outside of scheduled contact hours.
experience: Past teaching experience is the more relevant criterion than the need to acquire experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum: PhD completed or in progress in social work or related field. Extensive knowledge and work experience in the area of Gerontology.  Knowledge of social work theories, and interventions relevant to the experience of family caregiving for older adults.  Sound understanding of the caregiving journey and how it is negotiated based on diverse social locations.  Preference may be given to candidates who have the most experience teaching in this area at the graduate level.
qualifications_preferred: PhD completed or in progress in social work (preferred) or related field. Extensive knowledge and work experience in the area of Gerontology.  Knowledge of social work theories, and interventions relevant to the experience of family caregiving for older adults.  Sound understanding of the caregiving journey and how it is negotiated based on diverse social locations.  Preference may be given to candidates who have the most experience teaching in this area at the graduate level.

application_procedure:

duties:
Assist the instructor with course delivery and student support. Duties may include:

* Holding weekly office hours and tutorials
* Supporting student learning in formulation science, interfacial phenomena, and materials characterization
* Assisting students with experimental design, statistical analysis, and data interpretation
* Supporting computational assignments involving data analysis and machine learning
* Marking assignments, project reports, presentations, quizzes, and examinations
* Providing feedback on student design projects
* Assisting with course administration and online course materials
* Other duties as assigned by the instructor
experience: Previous experience is the more relevant criterion than the need to acquire experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum: Candidates should be enrolled, or have applied for admission, to the School of Graduate Studies at the University of Toronto. Candidates should possess strong written and oral communication skills and a demonstrated commitment to teaching. Applicants should have experience in experimental characterization, quantitative data analysis, scientific programming, and computational approaches for interpreting physical measurements. Experience with image analysis, machine learning, automation, or data-driven engineering workflows is required.
qualifications_preferred: Preference will be given to applicants with demonstrated experience in one or more of the following areas: formulation science, colloids and interfaces, rheology, materials characterization, experimental design, data analysis, machine learning, laboratory automation, self-driving laboratories, or materials informatics. Experience with Python, MATLAB, statistical analysis, image analysis, or scientific computing is considered an asset.
salery: $53.92per hour + vacation pay. Should rates stipulated in the Collective Agreement vary from rates stated in this posting, the rates stated in the Collective Agreement shall prevail.

job_title: Introduction to Environmental Studies - Regular TA
application_procedure:
Please submit an application on https://taships.iit.artsci.utoronto.ca/env/login.

Applications should include: i) a letter of application, explaining why you are qualified for the course(s) to which you are applying, and ii) a CV (including academic and applied environmental experience). 

Please note: The letter of application should be combined with your CV, and uploaded as a single file, where you are asked to upload your CV. Applicants are requested not to apply to TA positions for which they do not have the qualifications listed under "Minimum Qualifications".
duties: Duties include leading tutorial sessions, grading assignments, tests and final assessments, responding to student email requests, and general assistance. Additional duties supported through the Writing Integrated Teaching program, which may include additional training, benchmarking sessions, and other activities.
experience: The need to acquire experience is the more relevant criterion than previous experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum: A University of Toronto graduate student who has demonstrated scholarly expertise in Environmental Studies: drawing from the sciences, social sciences and the humanities to explore major social, cultural, economic, regulatory, ethical, ecological and technological aspects of environmental issues.
qualifications_preferred: An interdisciplinary environmental background is an asset.

application_procedure: Applicants must submit a cover letter, C.V. or resume, and a list of references by July 6, 2026. duties:
As a Teaching Assistant for the Robert Gillespie Academic Skills Centre’s (RGASC) Program for Accessing Research Training (PART), the TA will design and lead a select number of modules related to introductory research skills. The TA will perform the following duties: develop and instruct assigned in-person and/or online asynchronous and/or synchronous modules, mark assignments, track module attendance, and answer module-specific student inquiries. The TA will work and meet regularly with the RGASC Director and RGASC staff. Modules to be facilitated by successful applicants include: 

-Conducting Interviews 

-Descriptive Statistics 

-Inferential Statistics 

-Interdisciplinary Special Topic 

-Lab Safety and Conduct in Labs 

-Leading a Focus Group 

-Special Topics in STEM 

-Special Topics in the Humanities 

-Special Topics in the Social Sciences 

-Survey Techniques 

-Transcribing and Qualitative Coding 

Duties of this position shall be performed at the campus on which the position is located or online using Zoom.

Applicants must have experience conducting qualitative and/or quantitative research at the graduate level. Applicants must have the ability to work effectively with and show tact when communicating with PART administrators, students, and other Teaching Assistants.
qualifications_preferred: Preference will be given to candidates with experience developing and leading PART modules and/or teaching experience in an undergraduate research methods course. This job is for an instructor in a large undergraduate course with multiple sections which may or may not have a designated course coordinator.  Each instructor will perform tasks including but not limited to: lecturing, conducting office hours, preparing class materials / tutorials / tests / exams, grading assignments / exams, communicating with TAs. If there is no designated coordinator, there is an expectation that the different sections of a multi-section course will function similarly: they will normally have the same syllabus and assessment plan, and will have common midterm exams and final exam. Problem sets and quizzes should be common, or at least similar/interchangeable, and should operate at a similar level with a similar approach.  In addition, all instructors are expected to work together with the other instructors in the course and to collaborate in creating the syllabus, determining the course structure, and creating and marking the final assessment.
 
Normally, classes, tests and tutorials will be conducted in person.

duties: The primary duty of this position is reviewing, grading, and providing feedback on teacher candidates’ assignment submissions. TAs will familiarize themselves with the course syllabus and assignment guidelines. They will meet with the TA supervisor at the start of the position and as requested over the course of the year. TAs are expected to familiarize themselves with the annotated bibliography and literature review assignment expectations and evaluation instruments so that they can provide sound guidance and feedback to teacher candidates. Duties may include preparing, reviewing and/or curating resources to support teacher candidates in the preparation of annotated bibliographies and literature reviews.
experience: Previous experience is the more relevant criterion than the need to acquire experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum: Candidates should possess a strong interest in, commitment to, and experience with teacher education and teacher-research praxis, built upon a foundation of experience working in/with schools. Candidates should have experience conducting qualitative research, as well as writing annotated bibliographies and literature reviews. They should have a demonstrated knowledge and practice of critical and decolonial theories, as well as critical and decolonial approaches to qualitative research. Candidates should possess strong organizational and communication skills (verbal and written) and have experience providing substantive and specific feedback on student work. Experience with academic coaching and writing support would be considered a strong asset. A demonstrated commitment to intersectional justice is required. An Ontario Teaching Certificate is an asset.
qualifications_preferred: Candidates should have prior experience with qualitive research, including critical and decolonial approaches to qualitative research, as well as preparing literature reviews.
salery: $53.92 per hour plus applicable vacation pay

duties: The TA will work closely with the pilot research course instructor to support teaching and learning in CTL7006. The focus of the pilot is autoethnographic research in school-based context. The successful applicant will assist with preparing course materials, facilitating student in-class learning and seminar style discussions and workshops, coordinating school-based observation opportunities, supporting school-based inquiry, meeting with students, and supporting student writing. The TA will familiarize themselves closely with the Research 1 course syllabus and assignment guidelines. They will meet with the TA supervisor regularly to plan and de-brief teaching and learning of this pilot section. The TA will provide students with guidance in developing literature reviews (searching, citing, synthesizing, making meaning of the lit, researcher positionality). The TA may also chair two sessions at the annual MT Research Conference in February. Duties may also include preparing and/or reviewing resources to support students in their literature reviews.
experience: The need to acquire experience is the more relevant criterion than previous experience in respect of this posted position.

duties:
As part of the Writing-Integrated Teaching (WIT) program, a writing-instruction initiative in Arts and Science, the Lead Writing TA (LWTA) will work with WIT staff and the WIT Unit Contact to support course instructors and TAs in selected courses, as well as support the embedding of undergraduate student writing development in the hiring department. 

The LWTA will perform the following duties: 
• Participate in mandatory training sessions throughout the year: o Summer training will be held online on July 14, 15, and 16, and in person on August 17. These sessions will run 1:00pm to 4:00pm (Eastern) o Subsequent mandatory training sessions will held in person throughout the year on September 25, November 27, January 8, March 5, and April 9 (Time TBA) • Read book chapters, articles, etc. on writing education pedagogy 
• Meet with WIT staff, departmental WIT Contacts, and course instructors to plan writing assignments activities and for tutorials, labs, or lectures
 • Design and lead training sessions and consultations for course TAs on writing-related topics, such as providing feedback to students on critical reading, writing, planning tutorial/lab activities related to writing, etc. 
• Develop and/or adapt resources on writing instruction (such as marking rubrics and assignments) for instructors, TAs, and students 
• Communicate regularly by email and in person with course instructors, WIT staff, departmental WIT Contacts and others to implement WIT activities
 • Collaborate with LWTAs in other units by meeting in peer groups and sharing writing instruction resources 
• Collect and analyze data on WIT (e.g., surveys, student writing samples, TA and instructor focus groups, etc.) 
• Document activities (e.g., help draft unit WIT goals, provide monthly updates, write a final a report about WIT activities in the unit, etc.) 

NOTE: The applicant must be available for approximately 40 hours of training and preparatory work with the WIT program (e.g., consultations with WIT coordinators, training and development sessions) throughout the year. Mandatory training sessions will be held online on July 14, July 15, and July 16, 2026, and in person on August 17, 2026, from 1:00pm to 4:00pm (Eastern); subsequent mandatory training sessions will held in person throughout the year on September 25, November 27, January 8, March 5, and April 9 (Time TBA).
experience: Previous experience is the more relevant criterion than the need to acquire experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum:
• Current PhD student with least two years’ experience as a course TA, whether leading tutorials/ labs, in-class instruction, or grading
 • Excellent oral communication and interpersonal skills, particularly the ability to work effectively with and show tact when communicating with administrators, course instructors, and TAs • Strong administrative skills, including ability to schedule meetings and training sessions, plan and document work 
• Strong time management skills with ability to meet multiple deadlines 
• Strong facilitation and presentation skills and/or experience leading/facilitating workshops 
• Evidence of superior writing skills in their discipline 
• Flexible schedule, willing and able to accommodate short notice requests for meetings with WIT staff, course instructors, and TAs

duties:
Math Background Tutorials (MBT) is a workshop designed to help incoming and current students prepare for university-level math and numeracy courses. It aims to help support a smooth transition from high school to university math courses. Students will prepare for university level university-level by working through a comprehensive review of essential concepts in algebra, trigonometry, and functions. Effective study techniques and problem-solving strategies will also be discussed. 
TAs will participate in a two-day training session prior to the beginning of the MBT Workshop. They will facilitate daily tutorial sessions following the MBT workshop methodology for the duration of the MBT Workshop and participate in a daily debrief at the end of each session. They will also grade quizzes and assist students in solving problems during sessions.

job_title: Invigilator, Services to Persons with a Disability
appointment_size: Hours of work are determined by the tests and examinations scheduled, therefore minimum/maximum hours cannot be guaranteed
application_procedure:
• Complete application form before closing date (June 21st at 11:59 PM). Applicants must be signed in to their UofT webmail account to access the form.
• Application form link: https://forms.cloud.microsoft/r/eAdfv4Mv1C. If you are experiencing technical difficulties with the form, email accessexams.utsc@utoronto.ca to report the issues you are having

Applicants submit:
• Resume is required; cover letter is optional
• Responses to two supplementary questions (posted on application form).
• University of Toronto student number and confirmation of registration status on ACORN
• Confirmation of availability for the period outlined above
• NOTE: Incomplete applications will not be considered
duties:
Incumbents will supervise the writing of tests and examinations written by students with documented disabilities who are registered with AccessAbility Services at the University of Toronto, Scarborough (UTSC).

Responsibilities include, but are not limited to, the following:
•Supervising in-person tests and examinations written by persons with disability-related accommodations; some remote shifts may be required
• Through active invigilation, maintain the integrity of tests and exams by adhering to departmental, academic faculty, and university policies and procedures
• Maintaining confidentiality and security of student and test/exam information
• Ensuring the provision of disability-related accommodations to students as authorized by AccessAbility Services
• Monitoring test/exam time allotments
• Handling student concerns/problems and referring to Test and Exam Coordinator where appropriate
• Documenting and responding to anomalous situations
• Preparing completed in-person tests/exams for return delivery
• Verifying student identification
• Maintaining required administrative records
• Ensuring the physical exam space, including supply cabinet, Invigilator work station, and students' writing areas, are kept clean and organized
qualifications:
• Applicants must be currently registered as students pursuing a degree at the University of Toronto
• Applicants must demonstrate a commitment to the principles of equity and inclusion
• Applicants must be available for in-person invigilation shifts
• Preference will be given to candidates who have experience working with persons with disabilities
• Experience invigilating tests/exams is an asset
• First Aid and CPR certification is an asset
• Completion of Mental Health First Aid and/or suicide prevention training is an asset
• Applicants are required to provide availability between the hours of 7:30 AM - 10:30 PM, Monday through Saturday
• A minimum of 10 hours of availability must be provided each week between Mondays - Fridays from 7:30 AM to 6 PM AND
• A minimum of 45 hours of availability must be provided during all final exam periods. N.B. Occasional Sunday shifts may also be required

Successful candidates must participate in mandatory training, which will consist of:
• Orientation training sessions: Thursday, August 27th, 2026 1:00 PM to 5:00 PM, Friday, August 28th, 2026 9:00 AM to 1:00 PM AND Friday, September 11th, 2026 9:00 AM to 1:00 PM
• safeTALK training (if candidate does not have the certification)
• New Invigilators are required to participate in a minimum of 3 in-person shadow shifts prior to the examination period
• New Invigilators are required to participate in preliminary in-person independent shifts after completion of their shadow shifts
Please note that all aspects of training will be scheduled between 7:30 AM - 5:00 PM Monday through Saturday.
Successful candidates must attend three final exam meetings to be held the week before deferred and final exam period begins. The meetings will be scheduled between 8:30 AM – 5:00 PM, Mondays to Fridays.
• Ability to respond effectively to emergency, difficult or sensitive situations while maintaining appropriate boundaries
• Ability to demonstrate integrity and professionalism, while remaining calm under pressure
• Problem solving and resourcefulness
• Attention to details
• Situational awareness and the ability to manage changing priorities
• Understanding and ability to apply relevant University and government policies, procedures and legislation
• Initiative
• Ability to collaborate with others and work in a team setting
• Organizational
• Communication (both oral and written)
• Strong computer skills, including MS Office; Experience with assistive technology and Zoom an asset
salery: $49.76/hr + $1/hr worked on Saturday and Sunday shifts + 4% vacation pay

duties: As part of the Writing-Integrated Teaching (WIT) program, a writing-instruction initiative in Arts and Science, the Lead Writing TA (LWTA) will work with WIT staff and the WIT Unit Contact to support course instructors and TAs in selected courses, as well as support undergraduate student writing development overall in the hiring department. The LWTA will perform the following duties: Participate in training sessions (with LWTAs from other units) in July and throughout the year (late August, early January, and an end-of-year event); Read book chapters, articles, etc. on writing education pedagogy; Meet with WIT staff, departmental WIT Contacts, and course instructors to plan writing assignments activities and for tutorials, labs, or lectures; Design and lead training sessions and consultations for course TAs on writing-related topics, such as providing feedback to students on critical reading, writing, planning tutorial/lab activities related to writing, etc.; Develop and/or adapt resources on writing instruction (such as marking rubrics and assignments) for instructors, TAs, and students; Communicate regularly by email and in person with course instructors, WIT staff, departmental WIT Contacts and others to implement WIT activities; Collaborate with LWTAs in other units by meeting in peer groups and sharing writing instruction resources; Collect and analyze data on WIT (e.g., surveys, student writing samples, TA and instructor focus groups, etc.); Document activities (e.g., help draft unit WIT goals, provide monthly updates, write a final a report about WIT activities in the unit, etc.). NOTE: The applicant must be available for approximately 40 hours of training and preparatory work with the WIT program (e.g., consultations with WIT coordinators, training and development sessions) throughout the year. Mandatory training sessions will be held online on July 14, July 15, and July 16, 2026, and in person on August 17, 2026, from 1:00pm to 4:00pm (Eastern); subsequent mandatory training sessions will held in person throughout the year on September 25, November 27, January 8, March 5, and April 9 (Time TBA).
experience: The need to acquire experience is the more relevant criterion than previous experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum: Current PhD student with least two years’ experience as a course TA, whether leading tutorials/ labs, in-class instruction, or grading. Excellent oral communication and interpersonal skills, particularly the ability to work effectively with and show tact when communicating with administrators, course instructors, and TAs. Strong administrative skills, including ability to schedule meetings and training sessions, plan and document work. Strong time management skills with ability to meet multiple deadlines. Strong facilitation and presentation skills and/or experience leading/facilitating workshops. Evidence of superior writing skills in their discipline. Flexible schedule, willing and able to accommodate short notice requests for meetings with WIT staff, course instructors, and TAs.
qualifications_preferred: Ideal candidate will have finished with their graduate exams and have extensive teaching experience, including as a TA in CLA160.
salery: $53.92 per hour plus 4 or 6% (if eligible) vacation pay

"The instructor is responsible for all aspects of delivering the course, including:

- Planning and delivering the lectures in-person as scheduled. 
- Handling course administration including: maintaining the course website on quercus, and developing a marking scheme, 
- Providing appropriate contact time outside of class to students, through office hours, email, the course website and/or the course bulletin board. 
- Preparing the breakdown of hours for TA duties in the course and supervising the TAs. 
- Ensuring that tutorials and/or labs are delivered appropriately by the TAs. 
- Managing the grading for the course, which is largely done by the TAs; completing any grading not handled by the TAs; includes the timely completion and release of grades and feedback to students throughout the term; 
- Managing the submission of final course grades (due May 7, 2027) 
- Invigilating the final exam if applicable. 
- While there is a lot of room for creativity in course delivery, instructors will be expected to follow the basic content and style used by the faculty members who normally teach the course, and must get approval from these faculty members or the Associate Chair, Undergraduate on any substantial changes to the course content or assessment methods. appointment_size: 145 hours
application_procedure:
1. Submit applications on-line through the TA system:  https://taships.iit.artsci.utoronto.ca/ant/login
2. Please submit a writing sample (individually authored and no more than four pages) by June 3, 2026 to Josie Alaimo through email to: josie.alaimo@utoronto.ca.
duties:
* The applicant must be available for approximately 40 hours of training and preparatory work with the WIT program (e.g., consultations with WIT coordinators, training and development sessions) throughout the year.  Mandatory training sessions will be held online on July 14, 15, and 16, and in person on August 17. These sessions will run 1:00pm to 4:00pm (Eastern) As part of the Writing-Integrated Teaching (WIT) program, a writing-instruction initiative in Arts and Science, the Lead Writing TA (LWTA) will work with WIT staff and the WIT Unit Contact to support course instructors and TAs in selected courses, as well as support the embedding of undergraduate student writing development in the hiring department. The LWTA will perform the following duties:
•  Participate in mandatory training sessions throughout the year:
Summer training will be held online on July 14, 15, and 16, and in person on August 17. These sessions will run 1:00pm to 4:00pm (Eastern)
•  Subsequent mandatory training sessions will held in person throughout the year on September 25, November 27, January 8, March 5, and April 9 (Time TBA)
•  Read book chapters, articles, etc. on writing education pedagogy
•  Meet with WIT staff, departmental WIT Contacts, and course instructors to plan writing assignments activities and for tutorials, labs, or lectures
•  Design and lead training sessions and consultations for course TAs on writing-related topics, such as providing feedback to students on critical reading, writing, planning tutorial/lab activities related to writing, etc.
•  Develop and/or adapt resources on writing instruction (such as marking rubrics and assignments) for instructors, TAs, and students 
•  Communicate regularly by email and in person with course instructors, WIT staff, departmental WIT Contacts and others to implement WIT activities
•  Collaborate with LWTAs in other units by meeting in peer groups and sharing writing instruction resources
•  Collect and analyze data on WIT (e.g., surveys, student writing samples, TA and instructor focus groups, etc.)
•  Document activities (e.g., help draft unit WIT goals, provide monthly updates, write a final a report about WIT activities in the unit, etc.)
experience: Previous experience is the more relevant criterion than the need to acquire experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum:
•  Current PhD student with least two years’ experience as a course TA, whether leading tutorials/ labs, in-class instruction, or grading
•  Excellent oral communication and interpersonal skills, particularly the ability to work effectively with and show tact when communicating with administrators, course instructors, and TAs
•  Strong administrative skills, including ability to schedule meetings and training sessions, plan and document work
•  Strong time management skills with ability to meet multiple deadlines
•  Strong facilitation and presentation skills and/or experience leading/facilitating workshops
•  Evidence of superior writing skills in their discipline
•  Flexible schedule, willing and able to accommodate short notice requests for meetings with WIT staff, course instructors, and TAs

duties:

•	Participate in mandatory training sessions throughout the year:
o	Summer training will be held online on July 14, 15, and 16, and in person on August 17. These sessions will run 1:00pm to 4:00pm (Eastern)
o	Subsequent mandatory training sessions will held in person throughout the year on September 25, November 27, January 8, March 5, and April 9 (Time TBA)
•	Read book chapters, articles, etc. on writing education pedagogy
•	Meet with WIT staff, departmental WIT Contacts, and course instructors to plan writing assignments activities and for tutorials, labs, or lectures
•	Design and lead training sessions and consultations for course TAs on writing-related topics, such as providing feedback to students on critical reading, writing, planning tutorial/lab activities related to writing, etc.
•	Develop and/or adapt resources on writing instruction (such as marking rubrics and assignments) for instructors, TAs, and students 
•	Communicate regularly by email and in person with course instructors, WIT staff, departmental WIT Contacts and others to implement WIT activities
•	Collaborate with LWTAs in other units by meeting in peer groups and sharing writing instruction resources
•	Collect and analyze data on WIT (e.g., surveys, student writing samples, TA and instructor focus groups, etc.)
•	Document activities (e.g., help draft unit WIT goals, provide monthly updates, write a final a report about WIT activities in the unit, etc.)

Demonstrated knowedge of the critical and litrary theory relevant to the course materials (as shown, for example, by dissertation research).  This course explores representation as a cultural and political problem.  Representation is both a means of depicting or constructing reality and a means of constituting individual and collective subjectivities.  We will consider literary and other modes of representation in their historical contexts.  Texts will span a broad historical range and may include: Homer’s The Odyssey, excerpts from the Bible and the Koran, Aristotle’s Poetics, Dante’s Inferno, portions of Auerbach’s Figura, Eliot's The Waste Land, Barthes's Mythologies, Mahasweta Devi's Imaginary Maps, and Foucault's The History of Sexuality, vol 1.

qualifications_preferred:
MA in Comparative Literature or a closely related discipline.  Demonstrated knowledge of critical and literary Theory relevant to the course.  Scholarly expertise relevant to the course material (as shown, for example, by dissertation research). The principal focus of this course is the complex engagement of literature and the arts with both colonial and neo-colonial encounters between, Europe, Africa and the Americas over the last five hundred years. Exploring world cultures through the prism of colonial encounters facilitates discussions about the genesis of contemporary politics of globalisation as well as the accompanying dynamics of exploitation and resistance. This approach will also help to elucidate the ways in which contact between different world zones have been pivotal in the development of new national and regional cultures. In the process we engage with a wide range of questions including environmental precarity, food security, cultural violence, religious alterity, revolutions, and the coming of age of new subjectivities.   The material to be discussed will be organized under four broad headings: The Columbian Exchange in Cinema and Art; Colonial Encounters and Latin American Politics of Identity; Fictions of Colonial Encounters in West Africa; Narratives of Colonial and Neo-Colonial Encounters in the Caribbean. 

Primary texts may include works by
Miguel de Cervantes, Juan Rulfo, Octaivo Paz, Carlos Fuentes, William Shakespeare, Joseph Conrad, Chinua Achebe, Chimamanda Ngozi Adiche, Ama Ata Aidoo, Derek Walcott, Earl Lovelace, Olive Senior, Jaime Manrique and Anthony Winkler.
